  • Earlier commitments: Introducing binding agreements earlier in the transaction timeline
  • Digital transformation: Accelerating digitisation of searches and local authority records
  • Standardised timeframes: Establishing clear national standards for searches and enquiries
  • Process transparency: Improving communication between all parties throughout transactions

Industry observers note that Scotland's system – where sellers provide formal surveys upfront and transactions become binding earlier – typically completes purchases faster with fewer collapses. Similar models operate efficiently in other countries, suggesting England and Wales' system could benefit from comparable reforms.

As the petition gains traction, homeowners and property professionals may wish to follow developments closely. While systemic change would require government intervention, the growing consensus suggests the current conveyancing process may need fundamental restructuring to meet modern expectations.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK conveyancing system is under fresh scrutiny as industry professionals call for urgent reforms to streamline property transactions. With the average home purchase taking around four months to complete – and nearly a third collapsing before reaching completion – concerns are growing about inefficiencies causing financial strain for buyers, sellers and property professionals alike.

Why the conveyancing system needs modernising

Warren Sanders, a Warwickshire-based estate agency director, has launched a petition highlighting how England and Wales' property transaction process compares unfavourably with international counterparts. Current government figures show residential purchases take approximately 120 days from offer to completion – longer than some military conflicts. The non-binding nature of transactions until contract exchange frequently leads to gazumping, gazundering and last-minute breakdowns.

These delays create multiple pain points across the housing chain:

  • Financial losses from aborted transactions (surveys, legal fees and mortgage applications)
  • Emotional stress for buyers and sellers facing prolonged uncertainty
  • Cash flow challenges for smaller property businesses
  • Reduced market confidence due to high fall-through rates

Proposed reforms to transform the process

The petition outlines several key areas where improvements could modernise conveyancing:

  • Upfront information: Making property details legally required before marketing begins
  • Earlier commitments: Introducing binding agreements earlier in the transaction timeline
  • Digital transformation: Accelerating digitisation of searches and local authority records
  • Standardised timeframes: Establishing clear national standards for searches and enquiries
  • Process transparency: Improving communication between all parties throughout transactions

Industry observers note that Scotland's system – where sellers provide formal surveys upfront and transactions become binding earlier – typically completes purchases faster with fewer collapses. Similar models operate efficiently in other countries, suggesting England and Wales' system could benefit from comparable reforms.

As the petition gains traction, homeowners and property professionals may wish to follow developments closely. While systemic change would require government intervention, the growing consensus suggests the current conveyancing process may need fundamental restructuring to meet modern expectations.

item.title: HMO landlord wins appeal against penalty
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Upper Tribunal ruling clarifies HMO "rack-rent" definition

A recent Upper Tribunal decision has provided important clarity on how "rack-rent" should be assessed in Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMO) cases, overturning a £19,600 penalty imposed by Waltham Forest Council. The ruling establishes that authorities must consider a property's actual use as an HMO when determining who qualifies as a "person having control" under the Housing Act 2004, rather than relying on hypothetical valuations based on potential lawful use.

Case background and tribunal findings

The case involved a landlord who had entered into a management agreement receiving £3,400 monthly from a company operating the property as an unlicensed HMO generating £7,000-£10,000 in rent. Key aspects of the tribunal's decision include:

  • The Upper Tribunal rejected the council's approach of valuing the property as a single dwelling due to its lack of HMO planning permission
  • Judges ruled the First-tier Tribunal had erred in accepting the "lawful use" valuation method
  • The decision emphasised that actual rental income must form the basis of rack-rent assessments
  • The tribunal warned against creating unnecessary complexity through hypothetical valuations

This interpretation of section 263 of the Housing Act 2004 may influence how councils approach future HMO enforcement cases, particularly where management arrangements or licensing status are unclear. The judgment suggests authorities should focus on demonstrable rental income when determining if someone meets the two-thirds threshold to be considered a "person having control".

Implications for landlords and property managers

Property owners and managers may wish to note several practical considerations following this ruling:

  • Management agreements should clearly define responsibilities for licensing compliance
  • Regular checks on property use could help prevent unexpected licensing issues
  • The decision may affect how councils pursue penalties in unlicensed HMO cases
  • Professional advice could be valuable when structuring management arrangements

While this case provides welcome clarity for some landlords, it underscores the importance of maintaining proper HMO licensing and staying informed about regulatory developments. The ruling demonstrates how tribunal interpretations can significantly affect enforcement approaches and financial liabilities in the rental sector.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Upper Tribunal ruling clarifies HMO "rack-rent" definition

A recent Upper Tribunal decision has provided important clarity on how "rack-rent" should be assessed in Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMO) cases, overturning a £19,600 penalty imposed by Waltham Forest Council. The ruling establishes that authorities must consider a property's actual use as an HMO when determining who qualifies as a "person having control" under the Housing Act 2004, rather than relying on hypothetical valuations based on potential lawful use.

Case background and tribunal findings

The case involved a landlord who had entered into a management agreement receiving £3,400 monthly from a company operating the property as an unlicensed HMO generating £7,000-£10,000 in rent. Key aspects of the tribunal's decision include:

  • The Upper Tribunal rejected the council's approach of valuing the property as a single dwelling due to its lack of HMO planning permission
  • Judges ruled the First-tier Tribunal had erred in accepting the "lawful use" valuation method
  • The decision emphasised that actual rental income must form the basis of rack-rent assessments
  • The tribunal warned against creating unnecessary complexity through hypothetical valuations

This interpretation of section 263 of the Housing Act 2004 may influence how councils approach future HMO enforcement cases, particularly where management arrangements or licensing status are unclear. The judgment suggests authorities should focus on demonstrable rental income when determining if someone meets the two-thirds threshold to be considered a "person having control".

Implications for landlords and property managers

Property owners and managers may wish to note several practical considerations following this ruling:

  • Management agreements should clearly define responsibilities for licensing compliance
  • Regular checks on property use could help prevent unexpected licensing issues
  • The decision may affect how councils pursue penalties in unlicensed HMO cases
  • Professional advice could be valuable when structuring management arrangements

While this case provides welcome clarity for some landlords, it underscores the importance of maintaining proper HMO licensing and staying informed about regulatory developments. The ruling demonstrates how tribunal interpretations can significantly affect enforcement approaches and financial liabilities in the rental sector.

item.title: Home repossessions edge higher but remain below long-term averages
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Recent data from UK Finance reveals a modest rise in home repossessions during the first quarter of 2026. The figures show a 3% increase compared to the previous quarter, with 1,250 homeowner mortgaged properties being repossessed. Although this marks a slight uptick, repossession levels remain significantly below long-term historical averages. The organisation has emphasised the importance of borrowers reaching out to their lenders early if they encounter financial difficulties, as support options are often available to help navigate challenges.

Buy-to-let repossessions also rise

The buy-to-let sector experienced a similar trend, with 810 mortgaged properties repossessed in Q1 2026. This represents a 5% increase compared to the previous quarter but remains unchanged year-on-year. Despite these rises, arrears levels in both homeowner and buy-to-let mortgages continue to remain low. At the end of the quarter, only 0.91% of homeowner mortgages and 0.47% of buy-to-let mortgages were in arrears. The number of homeowner mortgages with arrears of 2.5% or more of the outstanding balance stood at 79,110, down 2% quarter-on-quarter and 12% year-on-year. Buy-to-let arrears of the same severity totalled 8,960, reflecting a 6% quarterly decline and a 24% annual drop.

Historical context and lender support

UK Finance highlighted that current arrears volumes are substantially lower than during the global financial crisis, when homeowner and buy-to-let arrears peaked at 216,400 in Q2 2009. The organisation noted that most repossessions today involve older mortgage agreements, with over two-thirds linked to loans taken out at least a decade ago. Repossession is typically considered a last resort after other forbearance measures have been exhausted. Higher interest rates and ongoing economic uncertainty continue to impact affordability for some borrowers, though mortgage pricing has stabilised in certain parts of the market.

James Tatch, head of analytics at UK Finance, commented: “Mortgage arrears continue to decline for both residential and buy-to-let mortgages. While possessions have increased slightly compared to the previous quarter, they remain low by historical standards. Lenders are prepared to support customers who may be concerned about meeting their repayments, and we encourage borrowers to contact their lender as soon as possible to explore tailored assistance.”

Mary-Lou Press, president of NAEA Propertymark, added: “While it is encouraging to see mortgage arrears lower in Q1 2026 compared to the previous quarter, it’s crucial to remain mindful of future affordability challenges, particularly in light of global economic uncertainty.”

Overall, whilst repossessions have edged higher, the figures suggest that the market remains resilient, with lenders offering support to those facing financial strain.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Recent data from UK Finance reveals a modest rise in home repossessions during the first quarter of 2026. The figures show a 3% increase compared to the previous quarter, with 1,250 homeowner mortgaged properties being repossessed. Although this marks a slight uptick, repossession levels remain significantly below long-term historical averages. The organisation has emphasised the importance of borrowers reaching out to their lenders early if they encounter financial difficulties, as support options are often available to help navigate challenges.

Buy-to-let repossessions also rise

The buy-to-let sector experienced a similar trend, with 810 mortgaged properties repossessed in Q1 2026. This represents a 5% increase compared to the previous quarter but remains unchanged year-on-year. Despite these rises, arrears levels in both homeowner and buy-to-let mortgages continue to remain low. At the end of the quarter, only 0.91% of homeowner mortgages and 0.47% of buy-to-let mortgages were in arrears. The number of homeowner mortgages with arrears of 2.5% or more of the outstanding balance stood at 79,110, down 2% quarter-on-quarter and 12% year-on-year. Buy-to-let arrears of the same severity totalled 8,960, reflecting a 6% quarterly decline and a 24% annual drop.

Historical context and lender support

UK Finance highlighted that current arrears volumes are substantially lower than during the global financial crisis, when homeowner and buy-to-let arrears peaked at 216,400 in Q2 2009. The organisation noted that most repossessions today involve older mortgage agreements, with over two-thirds linked to loans taken out at least a decade ago. Repossession is typically considered a last resort after other forbearance measures have been exhausted. Higher interest rates and ongoing economic uncertainty continue to impact affordability for some borrowers, though mortgage pricing has stabilised in certain parts of the market.

James Tatch, head of analytics at UK Finance, commented: “Mortgage arrears continue to decline for both residential and buy-to-let mortgages. While possessions have increased slightly compared to the previous quarter, they remain low by historical standards. Lenders are prepared to support customers who may be concerned about meeting their repayments, and we encourage borrowers to contact their lender as soon as possible to explore tailored assistance.”

Mary-Lou Press, president of NAEA Propertymark, added: “While it is encouraging to see mortgage arrears lower in Q1 2026 compared to the previous quarter, it’s crucial to remain mindful of future affordability challenges, particularly in light of global economic uncertainty.”

Overall, whilst repossessions have edged higher, the figures suggest that the market remains resilient, with lenders offering support to those facing financial strain.
